turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: In case 15-1204, Jennings v. Rodriguez, Justice Alito has the opinion.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: This case involves three provisions of US Immigration law that authorize the government to detain aliens in the course of immigration proceedings. Respondent Alejandro Rodriguez brought a class action arguing that these provisions do not authorize the government to detain aliens for a prolonged period of time without an individualized bond hearing at which the government must prove by clear and convincing evidence that the class member's detention remains justified. Both the district court and the Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit agreed with that interpretation. The Ninth Circuit based its decision on a canon of statutory construction, the so-called Constitutional Avoidance Canon. Under this rule, a court should not hold a statute unconstitutional if the law can reasonably be read in a way that does not violate the Constitution. We hold that we have jurisdiction to decide this case and we now reverse. The Court of Appeals fundamentally misunderstood how the Constitutional Avoidance Canon works. It is not a license to depart from what the statute says and replace it with a different statute, but that is what the Court of Appeals did here. Its interpretations of the three provisions at issue are implausible. The three provisions are analyzed in detail in the opinion of the Court and I will not go into all those details this morning. I will discuss just one of the provisions for purposes of illustration and that provision is 8 U.S.C. Section 1226 (C). It states that the Attorney General "shall take into custody any alien who is involved in terrorist activities or other serious criminal offenses" and it goes on to say that these aliens may be released only if doing so is necessary for witness protection purposes. So the statutory language could not be clearer, no bail unless it is necessary to protect a witness. But despite this clear statutory language, the Ninth Circuit held that the statute requires a bond hearing if an alien is detained for more than six months and that at this hearing the government must prove by clear and convincing evidence that detention is justified. There is no hint of any of this in the text of the statute and the Canon of Constitutional Avoidance does not empower a court to disregard what Congress has enacted and write its own law. The dissent does not adopt any of the implausible interpretations adopted by the Ninth Circuit or advanced by respondents. It offers its own interpretation, but that interpretation does at least equal damage to the statutory language. The dissent begins with a lengthy discussion of the constitutional right to bail in criminal cases, followed by a catalogue of scattered references in our cases to bail on immigration proceedings. When the dissent finally turns to the language of the statute, it argues that the statutory term detain doesn't actually mean detain. Instead, the dissent believes that an alien is still detained if its freedom of movement is curtailed in any way, such as by the requirement to obtain medical treatment, to report at regular intervals, or to abide by a curfew. That interpretation flies in the face of every known definition of detention. It makes many provisions of the Immigration and Nationality Act and other provisions of the U.S. Code incomprehensible and it is directly at odds with numerous decisions of this Court, all of which make clear that someone released on bond is not still detained. For these reasons we hold that the statutory provisions at issue cannot provide the relief that respondents seek. Respondents contend that the Constitution entitles them to that relief, but we do not reach that question. We remand this case to the Ninth Circuit and on remand that court will have to decide whether it has jurisdiction to consider respondent's constitutional claims, whether the case can go forward as a class action, and if it reaches the issue whether respondent's constitutional claims are meritorious. Justice Thomas has filed an opinion concurring in part and concurring in the judgment, in which Justice Gorsuch joins, except as to footnote 6. Justices Thomas and Gorsuch conclude that no federal court has jurisdiction at this time to consider respondent's claims. Justice Breyer has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justices Ginsburg and Sotomayor join in full. Justice Kagan took no part in the decision of this case.
